title: Enable WSL workaround for all WSL systems
body:
Before this change, the WSL workaround for rootlessport was only enabled on podman-machine through a special "marker file". This change enables the same workaround on "standard" WSL distros.

The detection is kept similar to what systemd does: https://github.com/systemd/systemd/blob/v258/src/basic/virt.c#L657-L664

This is based on advise from https://github.com/Microsoft/WSL/issues/423#issuecomment-221627364

Nowadays, there might be "better" methods to detect WSL (I was pondering just checking the `WSL_INTEROP` environment variable...), some of them listed in https://superuser.com/questions/1749781/how-can-i-check-if-the-environment-is-wsl-from-a-shell-script#1749811, so we might want to pick a different method...

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
WSL workaround now applies to all WSL distros, not just podman-machine.
```

comments.body:
> I really really hate this

I hate this too. But the issue in WSL has been there for ages and I don't think it's high on the "things to fix" list for the WSL team: https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/4851

This wouldn't be so frustrating if you could select the kind of exposed port cleanly with rootlessport (bug or feature?):


```
$ podman run -ti --rm --network=bridge -p 8080:80 docker.io/nginx
$ ss -ltpn                                                                                                                          ~
State         Recv-Q         Send-Q                  Local Address:Port                 Peer Address:Port        Process
LISTEN        0              4096                                *:8080                            *:*            users:(("rootlessport",pid=766,fd=10))
```
This creates a dual-stack socket, which becomes an IPv6 socket in Microsoft's magic relay:
```
> netstat -nq | findstr 8080
  TCP    [::1]:8080             [::]:0                 ABHÖREN
```

```
$ podman run -ti --rm --network=bridge -p 0.0.0.0:8080:80 docker.io/nginx
$ ss -ltpn                                                                                                                          ~
State         Recv-Q         Send-Q                  Local Address:Port                 Peer Address:Port        Process
LISTEN        0              4096                                *:8080                            *:*            users:(("rootlessport",pid=894,fd=10))
```
This also creates a dual-stack socket?!?

```
$ podman run -ti --rm --network=bridge -p 127.0.0.1:8080:80 docker.io/nginx
$ ss -ltpn
State         Recv-Q        Send-Q                  Local Address:Port                 Peer Address:Port        Process
LISTEN        0             4096                        127.0.0.1:8080                      0.0.0.0:*            users:(("rootlessport",pid=1020,fd=10))
```
This, on the other hand, creates an IPv4 socket (obviously).

pasta works differently (saner?) here:
```
$ podman run -ti --rm -p 0.0.0.0:8080:80 docker.io/nginx
$ ss -ltpn
State          Recv-Q         Send-Q                  Local Address:Port                 Peer Address:Port        Process
LISTEN         0              128                           0.0.0.0:8080                      0.0.0.0:*            users:(("pasta.avx2",pid=1256,fd=6))
```
Since this is now a IPv4-only socket, Microsoft's relay only creates a IPv4 socket on the Windows side:
```
> netstat -nq | findstr 8080
  TCP    127.0.0.1:8080         0.0.0.0:0              ABHÖREN
```

> --network=bridge -p 0.0.0.0:8080:80
> This also creates a dual-stack socket?!?

This is because the because there is code to drop 0.0.0.0 again as it was some compatibility thing
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/021583285288c50fbe5af6561b63af5c4627fee3/pkg/specgenutil/util.go#L178-L190
Not sure if it is still needed, I tend to agree that we should allow `0.0.0.0`/`[::]` to bind to v4/v6 only respectively and only dual stack when no ip is given.

> pasta works differently (saner?) here:

Do you have a v6 address in WSL? I think pasta most likely disabled its v6 mode automatically then and therefore not use the v6 dual stack socket.

> Do you have a v6 address in WSL? I think pasta most likely disabled its v6 mode automatically then and therefore not use the v6 dual stack socket.

WSL2 with "classic" networking (NAT mode) has no working IPv6 connectivity, but a working IPv6 stack. When switching to the newer "mirrored" network mode, those magic localhost ports only work as IPv4 all the time, so the issue is less noticeable...

Upon further inspection, I think I can fix the issue in WSL itself, but I need to dust off my C/C++ skills and find some time and motivation to set up a Visual Studio build environment 😞 - I'm closing this for now.
